design_philosophy.core_loop: Win street races by outpacing opponents through acceleration, skitching (grabbing vehicle bumpers), combat, and trick execution while managing equipment durability and entry fees
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk-reward between skitching for speed boosts (dangerous, high reward) vs. safe skating; combat engagement vs. staying on race line; equipment maintenance vs. cash flow for race entry
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as an aggressive inline skater using urban streets as a race arena, wielding weapons, performing tricks, and dangerously hitch-riding on moving vehicles
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Combines Road Rash's combat-racing formula with inline skating culture and graffiti aesthetics; treats skitching as a legitimate (if risky) racing mechanic rather than purely stunt activity; emphasizes style (tricks, graffiti art) alongside competition

difficulty.continues_system: Password-based progression system allowing players to resume after successful races; no explicit continue mechanic
difficulty.checkpointing: End-of-race passwords save progress; races are discrete checkpoint units across 12-city tournament
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Difficulty scales dynamically: cars become faster and harder to skitch as player progresses; opponents grow more aggressive; player can manage risk by choosing which vehicles to skitch (police cars offer higher rewards but risk fines). Equipment maintenance creates optional challenge layer—neglecting durability causes in-race penalties (blowouts, hospital bills)

progression.upgrade_system: Equipment management system: skates, wheels, and protective gear wear out over time and must be replaced. Better equipment improves performance and durability. Money earned from races, skitching, tricks, and combat funds purchases between races
progression.unlock_mechanism: 12-city tournament structure; advancing requires finishing 5th or higher each race; each city represents a progression gate
replayability_hooks: Tournament mode supporting 3-8 players in series of matches
Multiple weapon types encourage different combat approaches
Trick system with judge ratings incentivizes skill expression on ramps
Dynamic difficulty scaling creates fresh challenges on replay

world_building_techniques: 12 real-world cities as race locations (Vancouver, NYC, San Diego, etc.) ground game in recognizable urban spaces
Authentic graffiti art created by Toronto-based teen artists (Carlos Zelaya, Zack Benwell, Nelson Garcia) establishes street culture authenticity
Inline skating community authenticity achieved through rotoscoped motion capture of skilled skater Troy Manering
Grunge soundtrack aesthetic aligns with 1990s street/skate culture
Traffic and civilian NPCs create living urban environment context

faction_indicators: Police cars as rival faction (pursue skater, can fine; offer higher skitching rewards)
Aggressive computer-controlled racers with weapons and escalating tactics
Graffiti artist collaborators represent street culture alignment
danger_signals: Worn equipment triggers 'blowout' events during races
Police pursuit and fines when skitching police vehicles
Hospital bills from crashes with degraded protective gear
Opponent weapons (nunchaku, chains, pipes, crowbars, whips, baseball bats)
Insufficient cash reserves preventing race entry (cash minimum enforced by merchant)
environmental_storytelling: Traffic cones indicate nearby weapon pickups
Vehicle variation (police, civilian cars) signals different risk/reward scenarios
Graffiti art throughout tracks reinforces urban street racing setting
Real-world city locations establish geographic progression and authentic urban culture
Ramps and tricks system encourages player to interact with environment dynamically
